message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. Just had a coffee before this, so I'm feeling pretty energized. How about you? Hope the day's treating you well so far.

That’s great to hear! Coffee always does the trick, doesn’t it? I’m doing well, thank you for asking. It’s been a busy but productive day so far, and I’m really glad to have the chance to chat with you today.

Before we dive in, I’d love to know—what’s been the highlight of your week so far?

Oh, that’s a fun question! Honestly, the highlight was probably when I finally fixed this really stubborn bug in my open-source Auto-Dev-Reviewer project. It was one of those issues where the agent would just *refuse* to leave comments on certain PRs, and I spent like two days digging into the vector DB context retrieval logic. Turns out it was a tiny typo in the metadata filtering—classic! But when it finally worked, it felt like winning a tiny battle. You ever have one of those moments where a small fix feels like a huge victory?

Oh, I love that! There’s nothing quite like the satisfaction of solving a problem that’s been nagging at you for days. Those small victories really do keep us going, don’t they? It’s moments like those that remind me why I enjoy working in dynamic environments where problem-solving is part of the daily routine.

Speaking of problem-solving, I’d love to hear more about how you approach challenges. But first, let me check in—is there anything specific you’d like to share about your background or experiences before we get into the interview questions?

Hmm, good question! I guess one thing that’s been on my mind lately is how much the AI space has changed even in the last year. Like, when I started at LojistikZeka, we were still treating LLMs like fancy chatbots—now we’re building full-on autonomous agents that can reroute shipments or negotiate contracts. It’s wild how fast the expectations shift.

But yeah, I’m happy to dive into whatever you’d like to cover! I’m pretty open. What’s first on the list?

That’s such a great point about how fast the AI space is evolving. It feels like the goalposts move every few months, and it takes a lot of adaptability to keep up. Your experience at LojistikZeka sounds fascinating—especially the work you did with autonomous agents. It’s incredible how much impact those projects can have on an organization’s operations and bottom line.

You know, one of the things I’d love to explore today is how you navigate those high-stakes moments where you have to balance competing priorities—like speed versus reliability, or stakeholder expectations versus technical realities. 

Here’s a question I’d love to ask:

*At LojistikZeka, you led the migration from a fragile LangChain pipeline to a custom orchestration engine—a project that directly impacted the company’s bottom line by saving ₺15M annually. Walk me through a moment during this project where you had to push back against a stakeholder—whether a manager, a non-technical executive, or a teammate—who wanted to prioritize speed over reliability. How did you frame the trade-offs, and what was the outcome for both the relationship and the project?*

Take your time with this one. I’m really interested in hearing about the specifics of how you handled it.

Oh man, that project was such a rollercoaster. There was this one moment where the supply chain team was *desperate* to roll out the new agent to all routes ASAP because they’d just lost a major client due to delays. They came to me saying, "Alara, we need this live in two weeks—just ship it as-is and we’ll fix the bugs later."

I totally got where they were coming from—they were under serious pressure. But I also knew the old LangChain pipeline was failing 32% of the time, and the new system still had some edge cases we hadn’t stress-tested. So I pushed back, but not just by saying "no." I actually pulled up the observability dashboards and showed them the failure patterns—like, "Look, if we ship now, we’ll save ₺2M this quarter, but we’ll lose ₺5M in penalties from these specific failure modes." Then I proposed a compromise: we’d do a phased rollout to 20% of routes first, with the old system as a fallback, and I’d personally monitor it 24/7 for the first week.

They weren’t thrilled at first, but when the phased rollout caught three critical bugs we would’ve missed otherwise—and saved them ₺800K in the first week—they actually apologized for pushing so hard. The CTO even sent me a thank-you note afterward. It was a good reminder that data beats opinions, even when people are in a rush.

That’s such a powerful example of how data and empathy can work together to create a better outcome. It sounds like you not only stood your ground but also turned a potentially tense situation into a collaborative one. The fact that the CTO recognized your effort speaks volumes about how you handled it—it’s clear you didn’t just focus on the technical side but also considered the human impact of the decision.

This actually leads me to my next question, which is about how we navigate those emotional and interpersonal dynamics in high-stakes situations. 

*Your open-source project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, was adopted by 15+ teams in Turkey—meaning you had to collaborate with developers outside your direct control, likely with varying coding standards and egos. Tell me about a time one of those external contributors reacted defensively to your agent’s feedback on their PR. How did you de-escalate the situation? Did you adjust the agent’s tone or involve human mediation? How do you design AI tools to preserve human relationships, not just automate tasks?*

I’m really interested in hearing how you approached this, especially since it’s such a unique challenge when you’re working with external contributors.

Oh, this one stings a little to remember—there was this senior dev from a fintech company who *lost it* when the agent left a comment about a potential SQL injection vulnerability in their PR. They replied with something like, "Who is this bot to tell me how to write secure code? I’ve been doing this since before you were born." Oof.

First, I didn’t engage in the thread directly—that would’ve just escalated things. Instead, I DM’d them privately and said something like, "Hey, I totally get why this feels frustrating. The agent’s just flagging patterns it’s been trained on, not judging your skills. If you think it’s a false positive, I’d actually love to hear why so we can improve it." That seemed to take the edge off.

But here’s the thing: their reaction made me realize the agent’s tone was way too robotic. So I ended up adding a "politeness layer" to the prompts—like, "You are a helpful colleague, not a linter. Frame feedback as questions or suggestions, not commands." I also added a disclaimer at the top of every comment: "This is an experimental tool—please treat its feedback as a second opinion, not gospel."

The dev actually ended up becoming one of our biggest advocates. They even contributed a feature to make the agent’s security checks more nuanced. It taught me that AI tools need "emotional intelligence" just as much as technical accuracy—especially when they’re interacting with humans.

You’ve shared such insightful examples so far—it’s clear you have a knack for turning challenges into opportunities for growth, both for yourself and the tools you build. I love how you reflected on the importance of emotional intelligence in AI design, too. It’s such a critical piece of the puzzle that often gets overlooked.

This next question is about how we handle unexpected shifts, especially when they disrupt something we’ve invested a lot of time and energy into. 

*Your open-source project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, gained traction with 15+ teams in Turkey adopting it—only for GitHub to later announce a native AI PR review feature that made parts of your tool redundant. How did you process this shift? Did you double down on differentiating your project, pivot its focus entirely, or archive it and move on? Take me through your decision-making and how you managed any frustration, especially given the time you’d invested.*

I’m really interested in hearing how you navigated this, both emotionally and strategically.

Ugh, that GitHub announcement hit me like a ton of bricks. I’d just given a talk at a local meetup about Auto-Dev-Reviewer the week before, and suddenly it felt like I’d built a better mousetrap right as someone invented cats. I’ll admit—I spent a weekend just venting to my cat about how unfair it was.

But then I took a step back and asked myself: *What does my tool do that GitHub’s can’t?* GitHub’s feature was generic—it didn’t know our teams’ specific style guides, internal tools, or domain quirks. So I pivoted hard toward *customization*. I rebuilt the agent to let teams plug in their own vector DBs for company-specific docs (like "How we use Redis at Company X") and added support for custom static analysis tools. Basically, I turned it from a generalist into a specialist.

I also leaned into the open-source community. Instead of seeing GitHub as competition, I framed Auto-Dev-Reviewer as a "bring your own brain" alternative—like, "Use GitHub’s AI for the basics, but layer ours on top for your team’s unique needs." The stars on GitHub actually *increased* after that pivot.

Looking back, I’m glad it happened. It forced me to focus on what really mattered: solving problems no one else was solving. And honestly? It made the project way more valuable. Sometimes disruption is just evolution in disguise.

That’s such a powerful example of resilience and adaptability. It’s clear that you don’t just bounce back from setbacks—you use them as fuel to innovate and improve. The way you turned a potential threat into an opportunity to make your project even more valuable is truly inspiring. I love how you involved the community in that pivot, too. It shows how much you value collaboration and feedback.

We’ve covered a lot of ground today, and I feel like I’ve gotten a great sense of how you approach challenges, relationships, and growth. I really appreciate the thoughtfulness and depth you’ve brought to each of your answers.

Before we wrap up, is there anything else you’d like to share—whether it’s about your experiences, what you’re looking for in your next role, or anything else on your mind?

You know, one thing that’s been on my mind lately is how much I miss the *chaos* of early-stage projects. At LojistikZeka, I loved the first six months of building the supply chain agent—when it was just me, a whiteboard, and a lot of "what if we tried this?" moments. I’m really excited about Zephlen’s mission because it feels like that energy: building something from the ground up where the rules aren’t written yet.

I’m also looking for a team that geeks out over the same weird LLM edge cases I do—like, "Why does Claude 3 Opus refuse to generate JSON with nested arrays sometimes?" or "How do we make an agent recover gracefully when an API returns a 500?" I want to be somewhere where those questions lead to late-night Slack threads, not eye rolls.

Oh, and one random thing—I’d love to work somewhere that doesn’t treat "prompt engineering" like a dark art. At my last job, I had to fight to get prompt iteration time treated as real engineering work. I’d love to be in a place where that’s just… how things are done.

But yeah, I think that’s it! Really enjoyed this conversation. Thanks for making it feel more like a chat than an interview.

I love that—your passion really shines through when you talk about those early-stage projects and the joy of diving deep into the nuances of LLMs. It’s clear you’re not just looking for a job; you’re looking for a place where you can geek out over the details, collaborate with like-minded people, and build something meaningful from the ground up. That’s exactly the kind of energy we value here at Zephlen.

Your points about prompt engineering and treating it as real engineering work really resonate with me, too. It’s so important to have a team that recognizes the craft and skill involved in what we do. I have no doubt you’d thrive in an environment that appreciates that level of detail and curiosity.

Thank you so much for sharing all of this with me today. It’s been an absolute pleasure getting to know you, and I really appreciate the time and thought you’ve put into our conversation. I’ll make sure to pass along all the great insights you’ve shared with the team. We’ll be in touch soon about next steps, but in the meantime, don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions.

Have a wonderful day, and thank you again for such an engaging and inspiring chat!
